comments.author: minimaxir
comments.textRawHtml:
The developer's guide (https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/latest-model) has some interesting semantic tips for using the model:

> Intent understanding: GPT-5.6 can better infer the user’s underlying goal and intended level of work without you specifying every step. Continue to state important constraints, approval boundaries, and success criteria explicitly.

> Original image detail: GPT-5.6 preserves the original dimensions of images sent with original or auto detail instead of resizing them to a patch budget or pixel-dimension limit.

> Use shorter prompts: In internal evaluations, replacing long, explicit system prompts with minimal prompts improved scores by roughly 10–15%, while reducing total tokens by 41–66% and cost by 33–67%.

> Avoid generic brevity instructions: GPT-5.6 is more sensitive than GPT-5.5 to instructions such as “Be concise,” “Keep it short,” or “Use minimal text.”

> Control warmth: GPT-5.6 does not become meaningfully better when prompted to be broadly friendlier or more empathetic.
comments.textZhHtml:
开发人员指南 (https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/latest-model) 提供了一些使用该模型的有趣语义提示：

> 意图理解：GPT-5.6 可以更好地推断用户的根本目标和预期的工作级别，而无需您指定每个步骤。继续明确说明重要的约束、批准边界和成功标准。

> 原始图像细节：GPT-5.6 保留以原始或自动细节发送的图像的原始尺寸，而不是将其大小调整为补丁预算或像素尺寸限制。

> 使用较短的提示：在内部评估中，用最少的提示替换长而明确的系统提示，可将分数提高大约 10-15%，同时将总代币减少 41-66%，成本减少33–67%。

> 避免通用的简洁指令：GPT-5.6 比 GPT-5.5 对“简洁”、“保持简短”或“使用最少文本”等指令更敏感。

> 控制热情：当被提示更加友好或更富有同理心时，GPT-5.6 并不会变得明显更好。

children.author: ravenstine
children.textRawHtml:
> Avoid generic brevity instructions

That part is confusing because it's not like they provide an example of how default GPT-5.6 output compares with GPT-5.5 both with default output and prompted for brevity. Whenever I use such prompts, it's usually because I want the model to give me the gist in a few sentences. I'd be stunned if GPT-5.6 was that concise by default. I would think that could "break" a lot of things for developers who didn't know to make prompt changes after upgrading to 5.6. What if you were expecting GPT to be as wordy as it usually is? Then suddenly your output is not wordy enough?

Smells like OpenAI trying its best to stave off financial armageddon for another few months. Then again, I'm not sure why they chose to waste so much output computation on verbal diarrhea all this time up to now.
children.textZhHtml:
> 避免通用的简洁说明

这部分令人困惑，因为它们并没有提供默认 GPT-5.6 输出与 GPT-5.5 的比较示例，两者均具有默认输出并提示简洁。 每当我使用此类提示时，通常是因为我希望模型用几句话告诉我要点。 如果 GPT-5.6 默认情况下如此简洁，我会感到震惊。 我认为对于那些不知道升级到 5.6 后立即进行更改的开发人员来说，这可能会“破坏”很多事情。 如果您期望 GPT 像平常一样冗长怎么办？ 然后突然间你的输出不够冗长？

闻起来就像 OpenAI 尽力在接下来的几个月里避免金融世界末日。 话又说回来，我不确定为什么他们选择在言语腹泻上浪费如此多的输出计算，到目前为止。

children.author: tshaddox
children.textRawHtml:
If you conceptualize this as “there is an appropriate amount of brevity for each situation” then it would be expected for a better model to use different amounts of brevity if it gets better at determining the appropriate amount.

My view is that popular models by default output wildly excessive amounts of prose for nearly every use case, so if this changes in a new model that’s a pure win.
children.textZhHtml:
如果您将其概念化为“每种情况都有适当的简洁性”，那么如果更好的模型能够更好地确定适当的简洁性，那么预计会使用不同的简洁性。

我的观点是，流行的模型默认情况下会为几乎每个用例输出大量的散文，因此，如果在新模型中改变这种情况，那就是纯粹的胜利。

children.author: ComputerGuru
children.textRawHtml:
> wildly excessive amounts of prose

Not just prose. I think this is part of the reason why you see ridiculous code with insane error handling and type checking even for impossible cases.
children.textZhHtml:
> 大量的散文

不仅仅是散文。我认为这就是为什么您会看到荒谬的代码，即使在不可能的情况下也有疯狂的错误处理和类型检查。

children.author: andai
children.textRawHtml:
This is one reason I switched back to Claude after testing various alternatives a few months ago. Claude ended up writing much more elegant code.

Although I was surprised that I could get very Claude like results from Chinese models though by just telling it to make the code elegant.

Reminds me of the old days with art AI where you had to put "+good -bad" in the prompt because otherwise it would assume you just wanted random quality outputs, because it had been trained on random quality inputs...
children.textZhHtml:
这是我几个月前测试了各种替代方案后转回 Claude 的原因之一。 Claude 最终写出了更优雅的代码。

尽管我很惊讶我可以从中国模型中得到非常像 Claude 的结果，但只需告诉它使代码变得优雅。

让我想起过去的艺术人工智能时代，你必须在提示中输入“+good -bad”，否则它会假设你只想要随机质量输出，因为它已经接受了随机质量输入的训练......

children.author: daveguy
children.textRawHtml: The models don't get better, except when a new one is released. Their performance depends solely on the model training before release and how well you curate the context you feed it. That's it. Contrary to popular belief these things are not intelligent.
children.textZhHtml: 除非发布新模型，否则模型不会变得更好。它们的性能完全取决于发布前的模型训练以及您对所提供的上下文的管理程度。就是这样。与普遍看法相反，这些东西并不智能。

children.author: coldtea
children.textRawHtml:
>The models don't get better, except when a new one is released. Their performance depends solely on the model training before release and how well you curate the context you feed it. That's it.

Not quite. The hosting side can change reasoning budgets (or re-assign what terms like "high" means), temperature and other decoding parameters, output length limits, finetune internal "hidden" prompt, latency optimizations, finetune attention algorithms, even change quantization - all still serving as the same model.

We know (or suspect) Anthropic frequently nerfs models while keeping their name and version the same.
children.textZhHtml:
>除非发布新模型，否则模型不会变得更好。它们的性能完全取决于发布前的模型训练以及您对所提供的上下文的管理程度。就是这样。

不完全是。托管方可以更改推理预算（或重新分配“高”等术语的含义）、温度和其他解码参数、输出长度限制、微调内部“隐藏”提示、延迟优化、微调注意算法，甚至更改量化 - 所有这些仍然充当相同的模型。

我们知道（或怀疑）人为经常削弱模型，同时保持其名称和版本相同。

children.author: daveguy
children.textRawHtml: Right. They can do all those things. And none of that will make it smart or able to learn new things. The underlying model is just an llm. But judging from the downvotes, it seems AI folks get upset when someone talks honestly about their precious piles of matrix multiplication.
children.textZhHtml: 对。他们可以做所有这些事情。所有这些都不会使其变得聪明或能够学习新事物。底层模型只是一个 llm。但从反对票来看，当有人诚实地谈论他们宝贵的矩阵乘法时，人工智能人们似乎会感到不安。

children.author: sureMan6
children.textRawHtml: Might bother you to use anthropomorphic terminology like smart and learning but they are capable of producing work that traditionally required human intelligence and the whole point of gpt 3 was the ability to "learn", you can give it an example of an invented brand new coding language and it can write working code in that language
children.textZhHtml: 可能会打扰您使用智能和学习等拟人化术语，但它们能够产生传统上需要人类智能的工作，而 gpt 3 的全部要点就是“学习”的能力，您可以给它一个发明的全新编码语言的示例，它可以用该语言编写工作代码

children.author: kmacdough
children.textRawHtml:
Context is not the same as learning. It's easy to conflate because they're tightly coupled in our brains.

The underlying structure and tuning of the LLM are entirely unchanged by context. It merely affects the attention and activation of the network. The LLM will not be able to work with this hypothetical new language unless it is in context. This does not fit the computational meaning of learning.

Smart is not a well defined term. Nor is it's general idea formally understood. Use it freely, but you won't be saying anything meaningful unless you define your usage.
children.textZhHtml:
情境与学习不同。很容易混淆，因为它们在我们的大脑中紧密耦合。

LLM 的底层结构和调整完全不会因上下文而改变。它仅仅影响网络的注意力和激活。除非在上下文中，否则法学硕士将无法使用这种假设的新语言。这不符合学习的计算意义。

智能并不是一个定义明确的术语。它的总体思想也没有被正式理解。自由使用它，但除非您定义了用法，否则您不会说任何有意义的内容。

children.author: DiogenesKynikos
children.textRawHtml:
The LLM is the model + context. The output depends on both.

You're making an artificial distinction. The LLM sees a new programming language for the first time, and can immediately code in it. That's learning by any reasonable definition.

If you go past the context window, it forgets, which is a limitation of current LLMs. But as long as it learned how to code in the new language within its context window, it has gained that new ability.
children.textZhHtml:
大语言模型即模型加上下文。输出结果取决于这两者。

你在刻意区分。大语言模型第一次接触一种新编程语言，就能立刻用它编程。按任何合理的定义，这都属于学习。

如果超出上下文窗口，它就会遗忘，这是当前大语言模型的局限。但只要它在上下文窗口内学会了用新语言编程，它就获得了这项新能力。

children.author: xylenox
children.textRawHtml: Yep, people always forget that early LLMs were sold as "Zero Shot Learning".
children.textZhHtml: 是的，人们总是忘记早期的法学硕士被称为“零次学习”。

children.author: kmacdough
children.textRawHtml:
Sold as learning, but that was a marketing term, not a technical one. From a technical perspective, the LLM is not learning. Only reacting based on its original training.

You might argue that the systems we've built around them are learning in a way, as they strategically condense and save artifacts from past interactions to pass into the LLMs context. But the LLM itself, which is the source of the intelligence, is not learning. It remains entirely unchanged throughout inference. This difference may seem trite, but it has significant impacts over the long term behavior.
children.textZhHtml:
作为学习内容出售，但这是一个营销术语，而不是技术术语。从技术角度来看，LLM并不是学习。仅根据其原始训练做出反应。

您可能会说，我们围绕它们构建的系统正在以某种方式学习，因为它们战略性地压缩并保存了过去交互中的工件，以传递到法学硕士环境中。但作为智力源泉的法学硕士本身并不是学习。在整个推理过程中它保持完全不变。这种差异可能看起来很老套，但它对长期行为具有重大影响。

children.author: DiogenesKynikos
children.textRawHtml:
You're making a highly arbitrary distinction between learning and ... learning?

The LLM can immediately learn and start using new skills. To any lay person, that's what learning is.
children.textZhHtml:
你在“学习”和……“学习”之间做出了极其武断的区分？

大语言模型能够立即学习并开始运用新技能。对任何外行来说，这就是学习。

children.author: Nevermark
children.textRawHtml:
Intelligence can operate without learning. At a minimum inference and learning don’t need to be co-concurrent.

Not disagreeing with your point, but your terminology muddies your point.

But your point doesn't acknowledge that even with inference, there is a lot of room to tune the calculations. Multiple models, quantization tradeoffs are just the most obvious examples. Every architecture can be adjusted to increase intelligence/watt or other measure, even without further training.
children.textZhHtml:
智能无需学习即可运作。至少，推理和学习不需要同时进行。

不同意你的观点，但你的术语混淆了你的观点。

但你的观点并没有承认，即使有推理，也有很大的调整计算的空间。多个模型、量化权衡只是最明显的例子。即使没有进一步的培训，每个架构都可以调整以提高智能/瓦特或其他衡量标准。

children.author: jatora
children.textRawHtml: No thats probably because you misread what you were replying to and your comment was out of left field. They didnt imply models get better intra-releasally at all.
children.textZhHtml: 不，那可能是因为你没看清你回复的内容，而且你的评论完全不着边际。他们根本没有暗示模型在版本内会有所提升。

children.author: coldtea
children.textRawHtml:
You used the word "smart" now, whereas on the comment I replied to, you said "better".

Tuning those can definitely make a model respond better or worse.

So your claim (quoting 100% as written) that "Their performance depends solely on the model training before release and how well you curate the context you feed it" is wrong. Hence the downvotes.

Doesn't matter if LLMs are to be considered intelligent or not for the claim to be wrong.

> But judging from the downvotes, it seems AI folks get upset when someone talks honestly about their precious piles of matrix multiplication.

Often yes. In this case, it's more like they get upset when someone says something factually wrong, and then defensively changes the goalposts.
children.textZhHtml:
你现在使用了“智能”这个词，而在我回复的评论中，你说的是“更好”。

调整这些绝对可以使模型响应更好或更差。

因此，你的主张（100%引用书面内容）“它们的性能完全取决于发布前的模型训练以及你如何管理你提供的上下文”是错误的。因此，投了反对票。

法学硕士是否被认为聪明并不重要，因为他们的说法是错误的。

>但从反对票来看，当有人诚实地谈论他们宝贵的矩阵乘法堆时，人工智能人员似乎会感到不安。

通常是的。在这种情况下，更像是当有人说一些事实上错误的事情时，他们会感到不安，然后防御性地改变球门柱。

children.author: daveguy
children.textRawHtml:
> Often yes. In this case, it's more like they get upset when someone says something factually wrong, and then defensively changes the goalposts.

Oh give me a break. Show me one example of 1) any knob twisting that makes the underlying model better. or 2) any example of the AI providers twisting those knobs to do anything other than degrade performance for their own bottom line or safety.

The current post says: "it would be expected for a better model to use different amounts of brevity if it gets better at determining the appropriate amount."

When no, the model cannot "get better". It doesn't determine any appropriateness of response realtime except for the weights baked into it from the beginning and whatever context it can muster. If you cram enough guidance that it doesn't decide to ignore maybe you can make it more brief. But it (the model) can do none of those things.

LLM models are literally stupid by design.
children.textZhHtml:
> 通常是的。在这种情况下，更像是当有人说了一些事实上错误的事情时，他们会感到不安，然后防御性地改变球门柱。

哦，让我休息一下。向我展示一个关于 1) 任何可以使底层模型变得更好的旋钮扭转的示例。或 2) 任何人工智能提供商为了自己的底线或安全而扭曲这些旋钮来做任何事情而不是降低性能的例子。

当前的帖子说：“如果更好的模型能够更好地确定适当的数量，那么预计更好的模型会使用不同数量的简洁性。”

如果不这样做，模型就无法“变得更好”。除了从一开始就融入其中的权重以及它可以聚集的任何上下文之外，它无法确定实时响应的任何适当性。如果你塞满了足够的指导，以至于它不会决定忽略，也许你可以让它更简短。但它（模型）不能做这些事情。

LLM 模型在设计上确实很愚蠢。

children.author: dahart
children.textRawHtml:
Why did you drop the first half of the sentence in your quote? The qualification there is important context for the part you did quote. And why are you talking about “better” within a model, when the sentence you quoted was talking about 5.6 vs 5.5? The post you’re referring to did not suggest a single model could “get better”. You’ve made some incorrect assumptions.

Your comments are conflating multiple kinds of “smart” and “better”. You’re right that if all the inputs are exactly the same, it takes a new model to improve (ignoring non-determinism). But the knobs and context and harness change the inputs, and they do improve output, contrary to your claim. You’re failing to capture the distinction between what the model itself does and how the harness can boost the model’s performance. It is legitimately valid and fair to call improved performance “better”, no matter where it comes from.

This all gives me the feeling you might not have experience with or understand what’s happening in today’s harness development, and the degree to which it may be as important as the weights. There are in fact a lot of things you can do to improve a model’s performance on tasks & benchmarks, without changing the model weights. @coldtea mentioned a bunch, but the harness feedback loop, internal prompts, system prompts, skills, and requests for a model to try harder, and verify and validate it’s output all lead to improved performance, all without retraining.

I agree LLMs are stupid; they’re statistical token predictors. But somehow statistical token prediction is amazing and works much better than we imagined. The talking points about LLMs being stupid token predictors are fading now because they lack explanatory power for how good the models have become. The big surprise here isn’t about LLMs. It’s about language, and how much “thinking” and intelligence is contained in language. We don’t have a good grasp on where the line is between language and intelligence. LLMs have crushed the Turing Test into dust, and yet we don’t consider them intelligent. They often appear to understand what you ask thoroughly, can re-state it in different words, they can correct your misunderstandings or add nuance you didn’t see. All this because that’s what humans do and LLMs talk like humans.
children.textZhHtml:
为什么您在引用中删除了前半句话？那里的资格对于您引用的部分有重要的背景。当您引用的句子谈论的是 5.6 与 5.5 时，为什么您要谈论模型中的“更好”？您所指的帖子并未暗示单个模型可以“变得更好”。您做出了一些错误的假设。

您的评论混淆了多种“聪明”和“更好”。你是对的，如果所有输入都完全相同，则需要一个新模型来改进（忽略非确定性）。但是旋钮、上下文和线束改变了输入，并且它们确实提高了输出，这与您的说法相反。您无法捕捉到模型本身的功能与线束如何提高模型性能之间的区别。称改进性能是合法有效且公平的“更好”，无论它来自哪里。

这一切都给我一种感觉，你可能没有经验或不了解当今安全带开发中发生的事情，以及它可能与配重一样重要的程度。事实上，您可以采取很多措施来提高模型在任务和基准测试中的性能，而无需更改模型权重。 @coldtea 提到了很多，但是利用反馈循环、内部提示、系统提示、技能以及对模型更加努力的请求以及验证和验证其输出都可以提高性能，而无需重新培训。

我同意法学硕士很愚蠢；它们是统计标记预测器。但不知何故，统计代币预测是惊人的，并且比我们想象的要好得多。关于法学硕士是愚蠢的代币预测者的话题现在正在逐渐消失，因为它们缺乏对模型变得有多好的解释力。这里最大的惊喜不是法学硕士。这是关于语言，以及语言中包含多少“思考”和智力。我们不太清楚语言和智力之间的界限在哪里。法学硕士已经将图灵测试粉碎成灰尘，但我们并不认为他们是聪明的。他们通常看起来完全理解你的问题，可以用不同的语言重新表述，他们可以纠正你的误解或添加你没有看到的细微差别。所有这一切都是因为这就是人类所做的事情，而法学硕士就像人类一样说话。

children.author: daveguy
children.textRawHtml:
> Why did you drop the first half of the sentence in your quote?

Because this entire discussion is about the release of a new model, and models are fixed. Sure you can try to modify all the scaffolding around it, but the model is the model. It doesn't matter what you're trying to improve. You can only improve the peripheral aides. And the peripheral aides can't fundamentally fix the problems with llm models when they can't learn new relationships or facts.

You will always have to wait for a new model (like this one we are talking about) for improvements to the model.
children.textZhHtml:
> 为什么你在引用中删除了前半句话？

因为整个讨论都是关于新模型的发布，而模型是固定的。当然你可以尝试修改它周围的所有脚手架，但模型就是模型。你想改进什么并不重要。你只能改进外围辅助。而外围助手如果无法学习新的关系或事实，就无法从根本上解决 llm 模型的问题。

你总是需要等待新模型（就像我们正在谈论的这个模型）来改进模型。

stories.title: Apple sues OpenAI, accuses ex-employees of stealing trade secrets
stories.titleZh: 苹果公司起诉OpenAI，指控前员工窃取商业机密

comments.author: joshstrange
comments.textRawHtml:
Some pretty damning stuff:

> OpenAI also instructs new hires on how to avoid scrutiny when they leave Apple. For example, Mr. Tan warns them not to tell Apple that they have taken jobs at OpenAI, so they can stay at Apple as long as they can.

> Apple says it discovered a pattern of OpenAI recruits emailing themselves confidential information when leaving Apple, including Tan.

> OpenAI apparently used confidential Apple hardware information when approaching Apple suppliers, and tricked one company into using a "specific trade secret metal-finishing technique" for an OpenAI device by claiming it had Apple's permission to do so.

> Liu allegedly kept an Apple-issued laptop after departing the company and exploited a vulnerability to download dozens of confidential Apple documents while he was working at OpenAI.

Non-competes and the like are gross but what's described here isn't just "bring your expertise to OpenAI" it's "here is how to steal secrets on your way out" which is even grosser.
comments.textZhHtml:
一些相当可恶的东西：

> OpenAI 还指导新员工在离开苹​​果时如何避免审查。例如，Tan 先生警告他们不要告诉 Apple 他们已在 OpenAI 工作，这样他们就可以尽可能长时间地留在 Apple。

> Apple 表示，它发现了 OpenAI 员工在离开 Apple 时向自己发送机密信息的模式，其中包括 Tan。

> OpenAI 在与 Apple 供应商接洽时显然使用了 Apple 的机密硬件信息，并欺骗一家公司使用“特定的商业秘密金属精加工技术”来制造产品。 OpenAI 设备，声称已获得 Apple 的许可。

> 据称，刘在离开公司后保留了一台 Apple 发放的笔记本电脑，并在 OpenAI 工作期间利用漏洞下载了数十份 Apple 机密文件。

竞业禁止等行为很恶劣，但这里描述的不仅仅是“将您的专业知识带到 OpenAI”，而是“这是如何在途中窃取机密” out”，这更恶心。

children.author: Aurornis
children.textRawHtml:
It gets even worse. The person not only kept the laptop and used an exploit to download confidential Apple documents, they bragged about it to a contact who was still working at Apple who was also feeding him information:

> Liu allegedly kept an Apple-issued laptop after departing the company and exploited a vulnerability to download dozens of confidential Apple documents while he was working at OpenAI. He also maintained a relationship with Yu-Ting "Alyssa" Peng, an Apple employee who continued to give him updates on Apple's projects, vendor decisions, and engineering details. When Liu learned he still had access to Apple's systems, he texted Peng "LOL, I found out I can access the [network storage], so funny."

This is how you behave when you think you're so much smarter than everyone around you that consequences don't apply to you.

Whenever I leave a company I make sure everything that belongs to the company goes back to them and I wipe any access credentials or authenticator codes that might be on any of my devices. I can't imagine being so brazen that you'd keep the company laptop and then start using an exploit to download confidential information for your new employer.

Doing it at a the company that most aggressively enforces secrecy is even crazier.
children.textZhHtml:
情况变得更糟。该人不仅保留了笔记本电脑并利用漏洞下载苹果机密文件，还向仍在苹果工作的联系人吹嘘此事，该联系人也向他提供信息：

>据称，刘在离开公司后保留了一台苹果发行的笔记本电脑，并在 OpenAI 工作期间利用漏洞下载了数十份苹果机密文件。他还与 Apple 员工 Yu-Ting“Alyssa”Peng 保持着关系，后者不断向他提供有关 Apple 项目、供应商决策和工程细节的最新信息。当刘得知他仍然可以访问苹果的系统时，他给彭发了一条短信，“哈哈，我发现我可以访问[网络存储]，太有趣了。”

当你认为自己比周围的每个人都聪明得多，而后果并不适用于你时，你就会这么做。

每当我离开一家公司时，我都会确保属于该公司的所有东西都归还给他们，并且我会擦除我的任何设备上可能存在的所有访问凭据或验证码。我无法想象您会如此厚颜无耻地保留公司笔记本电脑，然后开始使用漏洞为您的新雇主下载机密信息。

在一家最严格执行保密规定的公司这样做更加疯狂。

children.author: taurath
children.textRawHtml:
> This is how you behave when you think you're so much smarter than everyone around you that consequences don't apply to you.

Its also how some folks act like when they've done something they morally can't deal with - their subconscious starts throwing all sorts of obvious signs up until they get caught. I presume this was done for a giant pile of cash, stock, and probably a promise that nobody really cares if you show up or not, enjoy your retirement.
children.textZhHtml:
> 当你认为自己比周围的每个人都聪明得多，而后果并不适用于你时，这就是你的行为方式。

这也是一些人在做了道德上无法处理的事情时的行为方式 - 他们的潜意识开始抛出各种明显的信号，直到他们被抓住。我认为这样做是为了大量现金、股票，而且可能是为了承诺没有人真正关心你是否出现，享受你的退休生活。

children.author: discopicante
children.textRawHtml:
These were long tenured and valued employees at Apple. They likely already had healthy pile of cash and stock.

Maybe it was the environment at OpenAI encouraging this behavior. Or, is this a particular set of skills some/all of the individuals mentioned were already well-practiced at?

I hope this case goes to court so we can find out.
children.textZhHtml:
这些都是苹果公司长期任职且有价值的员工。他们可能已经拥有大量现金和股票。

也许是 OpenAI 的环境鼓励了这种行为。或者，这是一组特定的技能，所提到的一些/所有个人都已经熟练掌握了？

我希望将此案提交法庭，以便我们找出答案。

children.author: ryukoposting
children.textRawHtml: This is the caricature of Silicon Valley made real. It doesn't matter how much money Liu had, it isn't enough. It will never be enough. The entire culture is fixated on maximizing growth. Whether that's growth of the corporation or growth of one's own wealth. The Reagan-era "greed is good" thing never really died here (among other places).
children.textZhHtml: 这简直是硅谷刻板印象的现实版。不管刘有多少钱，都不够。永远都不够。整个文化都痴迷于最大化增长，无论是公司的增长还是个人财富的增长。里根时代那句“贪婪是好事”的信条在这里（以及其他一些地方）从未真正消亡。

children.author: joe_mamba
children.textRawHtml:
> The Reagan-era "greed is good" thing never really died here (among other places).

"Greed is good" is the MO everywhere on the planet now. Everyone I know in EU is trying to cash in as much as they can while the going is still good because they feel the ladder constantly being pulled from them (layoffs and wage stagnation everywhere, housing costs massively outpacing wage growth, etc). Either from scamming their company or from scamming the government and welfare system.

And the compound the issue, the government trying to "fix" this only makes things worse as they just add more taxes on the honest working class people in their quest to tax "the rich" and give to the poor, which is only a populist measure for votes, nothing that actually fixes the problem since none of the globalist robber barons are affected by such policies as all their wealth is tied in a network of international foundations, charities, trust funds, etc. so when you add a 3 Euro tax on Aliexpress items, you're only gonna hurt the guy trying to repair his TV and washing machine, not the real estate magnate living in Monaco.

I don't think we can escape this downward spiral in any peaceful and democratic way. That's probably why the EU is pushing all these privacy invading laws lately, to catch and crush any public uprisings before they happen.
children.textZhHtml:
> 里根时代“贪婪是好事”的那套玩意儿在这里（以及其他很多地方）其实从未真正消亡。

如今，“贪婪是好事”成了全球各地的行事准则。我在欧盟认识的所有人都在趁形势还没彻底恶化前拼命捞钱，因为他们觉得脚下的梯子正不断被人抽走（到处都在裁员、工资停滞不前，住房成本大幅超过工资增长等等）。他们要么坑公司，要么骗政府和福利系统。

更糟的是，政府试图“解决”这个问题反而让情况雪上加霜。他们为了向“富人”征税并补贴穷人，只是给诚实的工薪阶层加税，这不过是迎合民粹拉选票的手段，根本解决不了实际问题。因为那些全球主义的强盗大亨根本不受这些政策影响，他们的财富全都绑定在国际基金会、慈善机构、信托基金等网络中。所以，当你对速卖通的商品加征3欧元的税时，你伤害的只是那些想修电视和洗衣机的人，而不是住在摩纳哥的房地产大亨。

我认为我们无法以和平民主的方式逃脱这种恶性循环。这大概就是为什么欧盟最近要推行这些侵犯隐私的法律，以便在公众起义发生之前就将其扼杀在摇篮里。

children.author: ryukoposting
children.textRawHtml:
> Everyone I know in EU is trying to cash in as much as they can while the going is still good because they feel the ladder constantly being pulled from them

Your friends probably aren't engaging in "greed is good" themselves, just responding to the economic conditions created by people who are engaging in it.

In the American cultural headspace, "greed is good" refers to a very specific brand of robber-baron capitalism. It's a doctrine that was first given academic legitimacy by the University of Chicago's school of economics, then put into practice in the 1980s under the Reagan administration, and nowadays its loudest proponents are Musk and Thiel. It's an economic theory that lionizes extractive behavior by the economic elite, with some bullshit trickle-down argument that's too convoluted to be worth explaining here.

It creates an environment where everyone had to do evil shit just to put a roof over their heads, a self-regulating race to the bottom. Your friends are likely just unwilling participants, a side effect of "greed is good" rather than doers of it.
children.textZhHtml:
> 我在欧盟认识的所有人都在趁形势还没彻底恶化前拼命捞钱，因为他们觉得脚下的梯子正不断被人抽走

你的朋友们可能并没有亲自践行“贪婪是好事”，他们只是在应对那些践行者所造成的经济环境。

在美国的文化语境中，“贪婪是好事”指的是一种非常特定的强盗大亨式资本主义。这一学说最初由芝加哥大学经济学院赋予学术合法性，随后在20世纪80年代里根政府时期付诸实践，如今其最响亮的鼓吹者是马斯克和蒂尔。这是一种将经济精英的掠夺行为神圣化的经济理论，还附带一些狗屁不通的涓滴效应论点，其逻辑之迂腐根本不值得在这里解释。

它创造了一种环境，让每个人都不得不干些缺德事才能勉强糊口，形成一种自我调节的逐底竞争。你的朋友们很可能只是不情愿的参与者，是“贪婪是好事”的副作用，而非践行者。

children.author: nerdsniper
children.textRawHtml:
When analyzing an optimization problem, the optimal values for the input variables are often found to lie somewhere between min and max.

It’s not clear that the person you replied to was advocating for one extreme, but even given that your strawman-esque assertion is probably true … I’d suggest analyzing a few other points in the domain beyond merely the two extremes that you contrasted.

Which range of the domain do you think we’d be most likely to find the optimal values for society? Do you believe that range might actually be inclusive of either limit? Are you sure you’d like to live in a world where the extreme limit of income inequality is manifest?
children.textZhHtml:
在分析优化问题时，通常会发现输入变量的最佳值介于最小值和最大值之间。

目前尚不清楚您回复的人是否提倡一种极端，但即使考虑到您的稻草人式断言可能是正确的……我建议分析该领域中的其他一些点，而不仅仅是您对比的两个极端。

您认为我们最有可能是该领域的哪个范围寻找社会的最优价值？您认为该范围实际上可能包含任一限制吗？您确定愿意生活在一个收入不平等明显达到极限的世界吗？

children.author: WalterBright
children.textRawHtml:
I'm fine with people creating wealth that becomes theirs. That wealth appears because other people place value on it. The wealth is not taken from other people. If you decided to cap it, it caps prosperity of the economy, because their wealth comes from creating what other people want. Why would you want to limit prosperity?

Keep in mind that Mush created 4000 millionaires the day SPCX was IPO'd.
children.textZhHtml:
我同意人们创造属于自己的财富。财富的出现是因为其他人重视它。财富不是从别人那里夺来的。如果你决定限制它，它就会限制经济的繁荣，因为他们的财富来自于创造其他人想要的东西。为什么要限制繁荣？

请记住，Mush 在 SPCX 首次公开募股当天创造了 4000 名百万富翁。

children.author: nerdsniper
children.textRawHtml:
I’m just not convinced that value creation and value extraction are well-linked when examining outlier cases. The nature of outliers makes it far more likely that value extraction would be much more likely to exceed net value creation. Even if for the more median percentiles, value creation and extraction are well-correlated.

I wouldn’t suggest a cap, but I would suggest very high tax rates and a more stern eye towards anti-competitive practices and negative externalities.

As a thought experiment:

Celebrating unbounded greed (the most “extreme” option) would be celebrating when politicians steal as much money as they can by manipulating stock markets and Kalshi and taking crypto bribes, taking kickbacks from contractors and blackmailing or strong-arming the other branches of government.

Celebrating unbounded greed would be celebrating selling poisoned food and telling ourselves that its the stupid people’s fault for buying melamine-contaminated milk without getting it tested by their own independent third party before drinking it. And then blaming the stupid people again when their third party testing lab that was trustworthy gets purchased by the same private equity group that owns the milk company and starts watering down or falsifying their tests.

Celebrating unbounded greed would be celebrating people whose wealth increased by neglecting expensive safety protocols, blowing up neighborhoods with gas explosions, and silencing them by intimidation, as well as blackmailing and bribing the judges overseeing any torts.

The more of this you allow and celebrate, the more your outliers grow and become a true bimodal distribution. Where the peak earners are, by necessity, extracting more value than they create.

The thing is, we have had a lot of things like this happen in most developed countries. There is a lot of corruption, and it’s not divided along party lines. Celebrating and glorifying unbounded greed is likely to lead towards a state where there’s less “rule of law” and more “rule by force”.

Relatively unbounded greed can probably work quite well in a society with a strong rule of law to protect against corruption, dangerous/damaging corner cutting and fraud… as well as an economy that actually meets the assumptions of a “free market”: i.e. no individual buyer or seller has enough concentrated power to significantly impact the supply-demand curve all by themselves.
children.textZhHtml:
我只是不相信在检查异常值案例时，价值创造和价值提取之间存在紧密联系。异常值的性质使得价值提取更有可能超过净价值创造。即使对于较高的中位数，价值创造和提取也有很好的相关性。

我不会建议设定上限，但我会建议非常高的税率，并对反竞争行为和负外部性采取更严厉的态度。

作为一个思想实验：

庆祝无限制的贪婪（最“极端”的选择）将庆祝政客通过操纵股票市场和 Kalshi 并获取加密货币来窃取尽可能多的钱。贿赂、从承包商那里收回扣、勒索或胁迫政府其他部门。

庆祝无限制的贪婪就等于庆祝出售有毒食品，并告诉自己，愚蠢的人们购买了受三聚氰胺污染的牛奶，而在饮用之前没有经过自己的独立第三方测试。然后，当值得信赖的第三方测试实验室被拥有牛奶公司的同一家私募股权集团收购并开始淡化或伪造他们的测试时，再次指责这些愚蠢的人。

庆祝无限制的贪婪将是庆祝那些通过忽视昂贵的安全协议、用煤气爆炸炸毁社区、通过恐吓使他们保持沉默以及勒索和贿赂监督任何事情的法官而增加财富的人。侵权行为。

您允许和庆祝的情况越多，您的异常值就会增长得越多，并成为真正的双峰分布。收入最高的人必然会获取比他们创造的更多的价值。

事实是，在大多数发达国家，类似的事情已经发生过很多次。腐败现象很多，而且不分党派。庆祝和美化无限制的贪婪可能会导致一个“法治”更少、“武力统治”更多的国家。

在一个有强大法治的社会中，相对无限制的贪婪可能会很好地发挥作用，以防止腐败、危险/破坏性的偷工减料和欺诈……以及一个实际上符合“自由市场”假设的经济体：即没有任何个人买家或卖家拥有足够集中的权力来显着影响供需曲线他们自己。

children.author: WalterBright
children.textRawHtml:
> politicians steal as much money as they can

I am not talking about people who steal wealth, which is illegal. I'm not celebrating or making excuses for thieves or criminals. I'm talking about people who create wealth via the free market.

I agree that externalities are a problem, as it pushes costs onto others.

> as well as an economy that actually meets the assumptions of a “free market”: i.e. no individual buyer or seller has enough concentrated power to significantly impact the supply-demand curve all by themselves.

The free market does not require that a large player be prohibited from shifting the s-d curve. For example, ever-cheaper computers shifted the curve. And that was good for us.
children.textZhHtml:
> 政客们尽可能多地窃取金钱

我不是在谈论那些窃取财富的人，这是非法的。我不是在为小偷或罪犯庆祝或找借口。我说的是通过自由市场创造财富的人。

我同意外部性是一个问题，因为它将成本推给了他人。

>以及实际上符合“自由市场”假设的经济体：即没有个人买家或卖家拥有足够集中的权力来单独显着影响供需曲线。

自由市场并不要求禁止大型参与者改变s-d曲线。例如，越来越便宜的计算机改变了曲线。这对我们有好处。

children.author: nerdsniper
children.textRawHtml:
From Wikipedia:

> For classical economists such as Adam Smith, the term free market refers to a market free from all forms of economic privilege, monopolies and artificial scarcities. They say this implies that economic rents, which they describe as profits generated from a lack of perfect competition, must be reduced or eliminated as much as possible through free competition. [0]

> The definition of perfect competition is when the following conditions all hold: A large number of sellers and buyers – A large number of consumers with the willingness and ability to buy the product at a certain price, and a large number of producers with the willingness and ability to supply the product at a certain price. As a result, individuals are unable to significantly influence prices. [1]

You are free to say that you personally don't care for "free markets" and use another term to describe the type of market that you prefer. But the textbook definition of "free market" was already defined 250 years ago as a market in which "a large player be prohibited from shifting the s-d curve".

0: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Free_market

1: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perfect_competition

-------------------

> I’m just not convinced that value creation and value extraction are well-linked when examining outlier cases.

>> I am not talking about people who steal wealth, which is illegal. I'm not celebrating or making excuses for thieves or criminals.

Why would anyone assume that the most extreme outliers wouldn't (generally) be breaking some rules that the mode follow; breaking those particular rules would help them become an extreme outlier. Wouldn't it follow that if some % people are breaking rules to their advantage and getting away with it, that to exceed even those other "cheaters" that the most extreme outliers nearly must also be breaking those rules? It's like watching Lance Armstrong beat all the other cheaters and saying "There's no way he could be cheating!"

It feels like a fantasy to put so much blind faith in all the most extreme outliers and assert that none of them are doing anything illegal. Should we not tax illegal behavior much more severely than legal behavior? Would that not be rational?

Also --- of COURSE you "celebrate / make excuses for thieves and criminals". Uber would be a fantastic example of a group of criminals that you celebrate and make excuses for. Before Uber, if someone (even a municipal taxi driver) tried to pick someone up at an airport who flagged them down like a taxi, they would go to jail if they hadn't paid for an exclusive "airport taxi" license. Uber arranged taxis for airport travelers all the time - and they frustrated law enforcement using actual Obstruction of Justice via their "Greyball"[2] program. You can make excuses for them that none of this was found to be illegal/criminal, but Ycombinator partners are on record saying that the Lyft founders did, in fact, believe that what they were doing could very well land them in jail [3]. YCombinator partners encouraged them to move forward.

But you'd celebrate the Uber/Lyft founders and the YCombinator partners (as would I, for breaking the taxi cartel system that was even worse for the median American). And you have not ever celebrated the taxi drivers who went to jail for picking up passengers flagging them down at the airport before Uber/Lyft.

Perhaps this is because, to some degree, "capitalist" society is somewhat like a "racist" or "sexist" society, except instead of determining an individual's worth based on their race, capitalist societies discriminate based on how much capital one has. If you control enough capital, the laws are very malleable - what's illegal or not is what those with enough capital decide should be illegal or not.

Should the Lance Armstrongs of the business world be taxed the same as those who actually are not doping, but whose names we don't recall? Given that capital accumulation yields political/legislative/executive power, that might ensure that the Lance Armstrongs get to mold the law and its enforcement to their desires.

2: https://www.nytimes.com/2017/03/03/technology/uber-greyball-...

3: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Hm-ZIiwiN1o&t=8m46s
children.textZhHtml:
来自维基百科：

> 对于亚当·斯密等古典经济学家来说，自由市场一词指的是一个没有任何形式的经济特权、垄断和人为稀缺的市场。他们表示，这意味着必须通过自由竞争尽可能地减少或消除经济租金（他们将其描述为缺乏完全竞争而产生的利润）。 [0]

> 完全竞争的定义是当以下条件全部成立时：大量的卖家和买家——大量的消费者有意愿和有能力以一定的价格购买该产品，大量的生产者有意愿和有能力以一定的价格供应该产品。因此，个人无法显着影响价格。 [1]

您可以自由地说您个人不喜欢“自由市场”，并使用另一个术语来描述您喜欢的市场类型。但教科书对“自由市场”的定义早在 250 年前就被定义为“禁止大型参与者改变 s-d 曲线”的市场。

I guess it does increase their cost, or rather your share of their hardware depreciation.

AI serving cost is apparently mostly hardware depreciation rather than operating cost (electricity etc), and if your large context request is occupying VRAM for some fraction of a second then you are paying for the depreciation that occurs in that time!

e. H100 costs $20-40K to buy, with a lifetime of maybe 3 years, and will only consume maybe $2K in electricity if run 24x7 for those 3 years.

e. H100 的购买成本为 20-40,000 美元，使用寿命可能为 3 年，如果这 3 年 24x7 运行，仅消耗大约 2000 美元的电力。

children.author: staticshock
children.textRawHtml:
Think of the entirety of the context (the full thread of conversation, all tool call output, etc.) as one message that's been submitted to the LLM all at once just so it can generate the next token (which is just the next word or even syllable.) Once that token is generated it is appended to the context, and the entire context is once again used to generate the next token. Keep doing that until the entire response is generated. The larger the context, the more stuff the model has to pay attention to as it generates each next token.

To use an analogy: imagine your friend is the author of an unfinished book. They die with 19 chapters written, and on their death bed ask you to write the 20th chapter. Assuming you're up to the task, you can only do this well if you take the time to absorb the entirety of what's been written so far.

This is how LLMs work. Context caching is an optimization on top of this, but it has its limits.
children.textZhHtml:
将整个上下文（对话的完整线程、所有工具调用输出等）视为一次性提交给 LLM 的一条消息，以便它可以生成下一个标记（这只是下一个单词甚至音节。）生成该标记后，它会附加到上下文，并且整个上下文再次用于生成下一个标记。继续这样做，直到生成整个响应。上下文越大，模型在生成每个下一个标记时需要注意的内容就越多。

打个比方：想象你的朋友是一本未完成的书的作者。他们死时写下了 19 章，临终时要求你写第 20 章。假设您能够胜任这项任务，那么只有花时间吸收到目前为止所写的全部内容，您才能做好这项工作。

这就是法学硕士的工作方式。上下文缓存是对此的优化，但它有其局限性。

children.author: londons_explore
children.textRawHtml:
There are various difficulties with renting GPUs, especially if your setup is very custom.

The competitor would have to port their training systems to your specific network architecture, system design, rdma Vs ethernet vs infiniband Vs nvlink etc.

Getting it running might not be too hard, but getting it running efficiently and making good use of all those flops will require considerable human effort and wall time.

Add that to the fact most frontier labs seem to have a single huge training run - and to my knowledge nobody has figured out how to distribute that training run between data centers effectively.
children.textZhHtml:
租用 GPU 会遇到各种困难，尤其是在您的设置非常自定义的情况下。

竞争对手必须将其训练系统移植到您的特定网络架构、系统设计、rdma、以太网、infiniband、nvlink 等。

让它运行可能不会太难，但让它高效运行并充分利用所有这些失败将需要大量的人力和时间。

添加事实上，大多数前沿实验室似乎都有一次巨大的训练运行 - 据我所知，没有人知道如何在数据中心之间有效地分配该训练运行。

children.author: loeg
children.textRawHtml: I'm not saying this as a hypothetical -- I believe xAI is already renting their GPU farm out to frontier labs. Whatever logistical challenges exist, enough of them seem to have been overcome.
children.textZhHtml: 我并不是说这是一个假设 - 我相信 xAI 已经将他们的 GPU 农场出租给前沿实验室。 无论存在什么后勤挑战，似乎已经克服了足够多的挑战。

children.textZhHtml: 当然，租用的 GPU 将用于推理，推理可以在任何地方运行，并且需要比训练运行规模大得多。

The EU is a farce, an undemocratic virtue signaling organization, and this is why:

- The Parliament voted against the first reading of this proposal twice in 2026, the first time they only supported limited cases for it, while the second time they actually defeated it fully.

- The Commission didn't care, and kept the proposal on the table by refusing to withdraw it.

- Once the Commission does that, the proposal goes on second-reading (despite the first-reading having defeated it) and it is established in a very PERVERSE way in EU law that to AVOID passing the proposal in second-reading you need ABSOLUTE majority which is incredibly hard to pursue (you would think that we would need an absolute majority to PASS a proposal that was previously defeated on first-reading, not instead needing absolute majority to DENY a previously defeated proposal that was again forced to the table).

- Furthermore, absences in practicality count as "No" on the rejection. So of course they scheduled the vote in the summer when notoriously there will be many absences.

By never withdrawing a defeated proposal they can effectively and in practicality pursue any agenda they want (it requires a massive mobilization effort to find absolute majority to defeat any proposal, especially when absences for any reason effectively count against rejection).

In PRACTICALITY, the Commission can pursue any agenda whenever and however they want, and throw the votes down the drain.

EU's democracy is lipstick on a pig.

您能为我们这些对欧盟情况不太了解的人提供更多详细信息吗？乌苏拉做了什么腐败的事？或者说她到底被指控了什么？她取消出庭日期已经多久了？

谁是这一切罪恶的策划者？她？还有其他人吗？

children.author: lgvld
children.textRawHtml:

didn't know about it but apparently there was the Pfizergate: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pfizergate
children.textZhHtml:
> 通过撤回一项失败的提案，他们实际上能够有效地……

之前不知道这事，但显然有所谓的“辉瑞门”：https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pfizergate

children.author: TacticalCoder
children.textRawHtml:
> What corrupt things has Ursula done? Or what has she been accused of, exactly?

I'm pretty sure that, for a start, backroom deals with Pfizer during the Covid pandemic. She's been accused of deleting evidence (like SMS) and pressuring companies to delete evidence if I'm not mistaken.

I don't know what GP means exactly but the EU institutions are a special jurisdiction: for example a belgian policeman cannot go inside the EU Commission and arrest someone without special powers. Source: I know an inspector who went in there and arrested an EU bureaucrat that happened to be a pedophile.

And it's well known that to be sure that the police cannot come and knock at her door with a warrant while she'd be anywhere else in Belgium, she's made herself an apartment build inside the EU Commission. So she's safe there.

> Who is the engineer of all this evil? Her? Someone else?

Note that that is another topic. And your tactic is a common, sneaky but obvious, tactic. One doesn't have to know the answers to those questions to point out wrongdoings when there are wrongdoings.
children.textZhHtml:
> 乌尔苏拉做过哪些腐败的事情？或者确切地说，她被指控了什么？

我很确定，首先是在新冠疫情期间与辉瑞进行的幕后交易。如果我没记错的话，她被指控删除证据（比如短信），并施压让公司删除证据。

我不太清楚GP具体指什么，但欧盟机构属于特殊管辖区：例如，没有特别授权，比利时警察不能进入欧盟委员会逮捕任何人。消息来源：我认识一位督察，他曾进去逮捕了一名恰好是恋童癖的欧盟官员。

而且众所周知，为了确保警察不会拿着逮捕令在比利时其他地方敲门抓她，她给自己弄了一套建在欧盟委员会内部的公寓。所以她在那里很安全。

> 这一切罪恶的幕后黑手是谁？是她？还是别人？

请注意，那是另一个话题了。而且你的策略是一种常见、狡猾但又很明显的策略。指出不当行为时，并不需要知道那些问题的答案。

children.author: mrtnmrtn
children.textRawHtml: « I’m pretty sure that » and « if I’m not mistaken » are not words I’m glad to read on HN for such serious accusations. I’m far from being on the commission side on those cases and think that if you want to make a serious case you have to be more rigorous with the evidence and things you say
children.textZhHtml: “我很确定”和“如果我没弄错的话”可不是我愿意在 HN 上看到用来提出如此严重指控的措辞。在这些案件中，我远非站在委员会一边，而且我认为，如果要提出严肃的指控，就必须在证据和言辞上更加严谨。

children.author: danielmeskin

children.textZhHtml: “他们”到底是谁？

children.textZhHtml: 她的名字是冯德莱恩。 “乌苏拉”对你的朋友来说还好，但对公众人物来说就很尴尬了。你也写“唐纳德”吗？

children.textZhHtml: 我有时给唐纳德写信。  如果您在正确的上下文中说唐纳德，我们就知道在谈论谁。  或者基尔或伊曼纽尔。公众人物的名字没有任何问题。

children.textZhHtml: 因为埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 这个名字，埃隆 (Elon Musk) 脱颖而出。

children.textRawHtml: So does Ursula
children.textZhHtml: 厄休拉也一样

stories.title: SpaceX wants to launch 100k more Starlink satellites for 100x the bandwidth
stories.titleZh: SpaceX 希望再发射 10 万颗 Starlink 卫星，带宽增加 100 倍

comments.author: digitaltrees
comments.textRawHtml: I have started to see what I think are star link satellites at night on walks with my kids. It actually makes me sad to see that on person owns the night sky and is changing the literal stars my kids will grow up with. It feels different when it’s the government that theoretically represents people but when it’s one person that feels truly depressing.
comments.textZhHtml: 我开始在晚上和孩子们散步时看到我认为的星链卫星。实际上，看到一个人拥有夜空并正在改变我的孩子们长大的星星，这让我感到很难过。当政府理论上代表人民时，但当一个人真正感到沮丧时，感觉是不同的。

children.author: rayiner
children.textRawHtml:
> It feels different when it’s the government that theoretically represents people but when it’s one person that feels truly depressing.

I worked on technology for years that the FCC effectively killed for stupid reasons. So it’s heartening to me that someone can still just do stuff and build things. It’s amazing. If you asked me 10 years ago I would have thought that getting something like Starlink off the ground would’ve been impossible due to red tape.
children.textZhHtml:
> 当政府理论上代表人民时，但当一个人真正感到沮丧时，感觉是不同的。

我在技术上工作了多年，但联邦通信委员会实际上因愚蠢的原因而扼杀了这些技术。因此，让我感到振奋的是，有人仍然可以做事和建造东西。太棒了。如果你十年前问我，我会认为由于繁文缛节，让像星链这样的东西离开地面是不可能的。

children.author: AlecSchueler
children.textRawHtml: People can still "do stuff and build things" while having consideration for environmental impacts.
children.textZhHtml: 人们仍然可以“做事、建造东西”，同时考虑到环境影响。

children.author: trimbo
children.textRawHtml:
The thing is, everyone's interpretation of "environmental impact" is different. To one person it can mean, "don't put the construction garbage in the river." To another it can mean "not one single Delta Smelt can be scared because of this construction."

And because it's so flexible, in states like California where we have aggressive environmental laws, it's leveraged as the NIMBY trump card. When it can't block a project, the process is used to make it inordinately expensive and take decades. One example would be the environmental studies for the CA High Speed Rail.[1]

[1] - https://ifp.org/fast-track-democratically-approved-transit-p...
children.textZhHtml:
问题是，每个人对“环境影响”的解释都不同。对于一个人来说，这可能意味着“不要将建筑垃圾放入河中”。对于另一个人来说，这可能意味着“没有一个 Delta Smelt 会因为这种结构而害怕。”

而且由于它非常灵活，在像加利福尼亚州这样有严格环境法的州，它被用作邻避王牌。当它无法阻止一个项目时，该过程就会变得异常昂贵并且需要数十年的时间。一个例子是加州高铁的环境研究。[1]

[1] - https://ifp.org/fast-track-democratically-approved-transit-p...

children.author: Spooky23
children.textRawHtml:
The deregulation stuff isn’t about nimby. It’s making nimby 10x worse by making it hyper local. That means poor people who are poorly organized get boned. State regulations tended to help with that.

I live in upstate NY, the rebuild of the GOP here is around hyper local issues, mostly apartments and solar. MAGA changed the discourse and allows the rabble rousers to say the quiet part out loud. (Ie bike infrastructure and apartments will bring poor black people to rape and pillage)
children.textZhHtml:
放松管制与邻避无关。由于过于本地化，nimby 的情况变得更糟 10 倍。这意味着组织不善的穷人会被束缚。州法规往往会对此有所帮助。

我住在纽约州北部，这里共和党的重建是围绕当地的超级问题，主要是公寓和太阳能。 MAGA 改变了话语，允许煽动者大声说出安静的部分。 （即自行车基础设施和公寓将使贫穷的黑人遭受强奸和掠夺）

children.author: Dig1t
children.textRawHtml:
Do you have any specific examples of how new state regulations actually eased the regulatory burden for building something? Adding new regulations at the state level almost never removes the hyper local restrictions, it just adds a new layer of compliance on top.

How can the solution to burdensome regulations be MORE regulation?
children.textZhHtml:
您是否有任何具体示例来说明新的国家法规如何真正减轻了建筑监管负担？在州一级添加新法规几乎永远不会消除超级地方限制，它只是在上面增加了新的合规层。

如何才能通过更多监管来解决繁琐的法规？

children.author: yummypaint
children.textRawHtml: In NC there is a state rule that bans HOAs and local authorities from stopping people building cisterns to catch and store rainwater
children.textZhHtml: 北卡罗来纳州有一项州规定，禁止 HOA 和地方当局阻止人们建造蓄水池来收集和储存雨水

children.author: digitaltrees
children.textRawHtml: I am in NC and didn’t know this. Cool. Where you be at?
children.textZhHtml: 我在北卡罗来纳州，但不知道这一点。凉爽的。你在哪里？

children.author: Dig1t
children.textRawHtml:
>State regulations tended to help with that.

A law banning certain types of regulations at a lower level I would not call “state regulation” I would call that deregulation.

I suppose I agree with you that the solution to burdensome regulation is to write new laws which forcibly deregulate lower level things. But calling laws like that “state regulation” is true only in the most technical sense. Your example is a textbook example of deregulation.
children.textZhHtml:
>国家法规往往会对此有所帮助。

在较低级别禁止某些类型法规的法律我不会称之为“国家法规”，我会称之为放松管制。

我想我同意你的观点，即解决繁重监管的方法是制定新的法律，强制放松对较低级别事物的管制。但将此类法律称为“国家监管”仅在最技术的意义上才是正确的。你的例子是放松管制的教科书例子。

children.author: [deleted]
children.textRawHtml: [deleted]
children.textZhHtml: [已删除]

children.author: omgwtfbyobbq
children.textRawHtml:
To be fair, part of the inordinate expense is just because it takes longer for the environmental reviews (costs are in expected year of construction, so pushing a project a decade into the future can increase costs by 30-40+% (inflation + interest) depending on the specifics, even if everything else costs the same).

That's why the cost estimates for CA HSR jump a bunch every time an administration hostile to it enters the white house.
children.textZhHtml:
公平地说，部分过高的费用只是因为环境审查需要更长的时间（成本是在预计的建设年份，因此将一个项目推到未来十年可能会增加 30-40+% 的成本（通货膨胀 + 利息），具体取决于具体情况，即使其他一切成本相同）。

这就是为什么每次对 CA HSR 怀有敌意的政府进入白色状态时，CA HSR 的成本估算都会大幅上涨。房子。

children.author: trimbo
children.textRawHtml:
> project a decade into the future can increase costs

A very good point.

I don't agree we can blame Trump for HSR though. 2/3 of the time that has passed have had Democrats in the white house. HSR is nearly all pure-California-style self-inflicted wound. And honestly it's just the most visible project California has failed with, there are many others. The one I'm personally angry about is Prop 1. We're now 12 years after, and have no additional water resources even broken ground. It's shameful.
children.textZhHtml:
> 预测未来十年可能会增加成本

这是一个非常好的观点。

不过，我不同意我们可以将高铁问题归咎于特朗普。过去 2/3 的时间都是民主党人入主白宫。 HSR 几乎都是纯粹的加州风格的自伤。老实说，这只是加州失败的最明显的项目，还有很多其他项目。我个人对第 1 号提案感到愤怒。现在已经过去 12 年了，我们还没有额外的水资源，甚至没有破土动工。这是可耻的。

children.author: rayiner
children.textRawHtml: It’s an fully intra-state train line in a state that has an economy bigger than France. California should be able to build an entire EU-style HSR network with zero regard to what’s happening federally.
children.textZhHtml: 这是一条完全州内的火车线路，位于一个经济规模比法国还大的州。加州应该能够建立一个完整的欧盟式高铁网络，而对联邦正在发生的事情零考虑。

children.author: omgwtfbyobbq
children.textRawHtml:
CA can and did recently, but that's money that will need to come from and/or could have gone to other programs/etc.

I think the most recent cut was $4+ billion in federal funds for HSR. It's going to come from cap and trade instead, but the state could have had both.
children.textZhHtml:
CA 可以而且最近也这么做了，但这些资金需要来自和/或本可以用于其他计划/等。

我认为最近削减的是 4+ 十亿美元的 HSR 联邦资金。它将来自限额与交易，但国家本可以两者兼而有之。

children.author: Danox
children.textRawHtml:
Agriculture uses 90% of the water in California growing cotton, alfalfa and almonds, which are all very water intensive, Humans watering their lawn, drinking water and bathing, use a fraction.

Note: The way they divide up water usage. They have a third category, listed As environment, but that’s misleading because the people who have the water rights can always use a lot more water at their discretion at any time. where as the common citizen cannot.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_in_California

https://www.ppic.org/publication/water-use-in-california/
children.textZhHtml:
在加州，农业消耗了 90% 的水，用于种植棉花、苜蓿和杏仁，这些都非常耗水。人类浇灌草坪、饮用水和洗澡只使用了一小部分。

注：他们划分用水量的方式。他们有第三个类别，列为环境，但这具有误导性，因为拥有水权的人总是可以随时自行决定使用更多的水。而普通公民却做不到。

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_in_California

https://www.ppic.org/publication/water-use-in-california/

children.author: omgwtfbyobbq
children.textRawHtml:
We can in part. Like you said, it took a ton of time because of environmental review, and that's on the state. With that said, construction didn't begin until 2015, and Trump pulled billions in funding in his first term in 2016 and did the same in 2024.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California_High-Speed_Rail

If CA had moved quickly, that wouldn't have been an issue, or at least as much of an issue, but because construction took so long to start, it was.
children.textZhHtml:
我们可以部分地这样做。就像你说的，由于环境审查，花了很多时间，这是国家的责任。话虽如此，建设工作直到 2015 年才开始，特朗普在 2016 年第一个任期内拉动了数十亿美元的资金，并在 2024 年也这样做了。

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California_High-Speed_Rail

如果 CA 行动迅速，那不会是一个问题，或者至少不是一个问题，但由于建设需要很长时间才能开始，是。

children.author: AlecSchueler
children.textRawHtml:
> The thing is, everyone's interpretation of "environmental impact" is different.

I know, that's why we've developed all of these systems of representation to discuss and come to reasonable regulatory standards.

But that's neither here nor there in regards to the point being made that people can still build things in a regulated environment.
children.textZhHtml:
> 问题是，每个人对“环境影响”的解释都是不同的。

我知道，这就是为什么我们开发了所有这些代表系统来讨论并制定合理的监管标准。

但就人们仍然可以在受监管的环境中构建事物这一点而言，这既不存在也不存在。

children.author: nradov
children.textRawHtml: In California at least we no longer build housing or infrastructure. Not much of it, anyway.
children.textZhHtml: 至少在加利福尼亚州，我们不再建造住房或基础设施。无论如何，内容并不多。

children.author: Danox
children.textRawHtml:
infrastructure building is a bad word in the United States of America currently, Unfortunately, our major competition in the world is building infrastructure like crazy, but there is still hope, Because once that high-speed rail system starts building west to east or even east to west, all those areas in the middle of the country will change their tune. They will all be fighting on getting a piece of the rock, or I should say a piece of the rail.

The only mistake California made was not including one of the adjacent states as a destination, be it to Las Vegas, Phoenix, or Portland, all of a sudden business interests and those crazy Republicans would be on board because they’re concerned about missing out on making money, which is basically God in America.

I may not live to see it. but once they start going interstate with high-speed rail anywhere within the United States, the tune will change. It’s just amazing that so many people are short-sighted about it. More of that short-term thinking humans are famous for.

Note: Those who think ahead long-term obviously have already bought land on both sides of the route of that high-speed rail line in California, and probably along the proposed route leading to Las Vegas in the future, and the same applies to any possible line to Phoenix or to the Arizona high plateau.
children.textZhHtml:
基础设施建设目前在美国是一个坏词，不幸的是，我们在世界上的主要竞争是疯狂建设基础设施，但仍然有希望，因为一旦高铁系统开始建设西向东甚至东向西，所有中部地区都会改变他们的调子。他们都将争夺一块岩石，或者我应该说一块铁轨。

加州犯的唯一错误是没有将邻近的州之一作为目的地，无论是拉斯维加斯、菲尼克斯还是波特兰，突然间商业利益和那些疯狂的共和党人都会加入进来，因为他们担心错过赚钱，这在美国基本上是上帝。

我可能活不到看到这一点。但一旦他们开始在美国境内的任何地方通过高铁跨州行驶，情况就会改变。令人惊讶的是，这么多人对此都目光短浅。人类以更多的短期思维而闻名。

注：那些长远考虑的人显然已经在加利福尼亚州高铁线路两侧购买了土地，并且可能在未来通往拉斯维加斯的拟议路线上购买了土地，这同样适用于任何可能通往凤凰城或亚利桑那高原的线路。

children.author: rayiner
children.textRawHtml: Blaming “republicans” for California’s inability to build a train line within California is an ungodly level of cope.
children.textZhHtml: 将加州无法在加州境内修建铁路线归咎于“共和党人”是一种不敬虔的应对方式。

stories.title: John Deere owners will get the right to repair equipment under FTC settlement
stories.titleZh: 根据联邦贸易委员会和解协议，约翰迪尔所有者将获得维修设备的权利

comments.author: Cider9986
comments.textRawHtml:
Shout out to Louis Rossmann for doing a ton of work on Right to repair.

He started a website called Consumer Rights Wiki to document anti-consumer practices.

https://consumerrights.wiki/w/Main_Page

He's also involved with FULU Foundation which has a bounty of 25k to get Ring cameras working without Amazon's servers.

https://bounties.fulu.org/bounties/ring-video-doorbells
comments.textZhHtml:
赞扬 Louis Rossmann 在维修权方面所做的大量工作。

他创建了一个名为 Consumer Rights Wiki 的网站来记录反消费者行为。

https://consumerrights.wiki/w/Main_Page

他还参与了 FULU 基金会，该基金会悬赏 25,000 美元让 Ring 相机在不使用的情况下正常工作亚马逊的服务器。

https://bounties.fulu.org/bounties/ring-video-doorbells

children.author: srejk
children.textRawHtml: I appreciate everything he stands for - he's on the right side of just about every issue. I just wish he could make more succinct and effective videos.
children.textZhHtml: 我欣赏他所代表的一切 - 他几乎在所有问题上都站在正确的一边。我只是希望他能制作出更加简洁有效的视频。

children.author: darksim905
children.textRawHtml: You really want more succinct videos than what he already makes? Although they may seem long in a modern day where people have the attention span of a gnat -- his videos are fantastic and to the point. He literally ignores a lot more complexities and drama surrounding a lot of issues. Does he ramble sometimes? Maybe, but the talks so fast at times that the video is over before I've even gotten half way through with my own research. And this is from someone who has followed him since he posted videos on spinning on your own VoIP server and when he was still based in NYC.
children.textZhHtml: 您真的想要比他已经制作的视频更简洁的视频吗？尽管在当今人们的注意力集中时间有限的今天，这些视频可能看起来很长，但他的视频非常棒，而且切题。他确实忽略了围绕许多问题的更多复杂性和戏剧性。他有时会胡言乱语吗？也许吧，但有时谈话速度太快，以至于我自己的研究还没完成一半，视频就结束了。这是来自自从他在您自己的 VoIP 服务器上发布有关旋转的视频以及他还在纽约时就一直关注他的人。

children.author: Zancarius

children.textZhHtml: 我个人认为，如果他这样做，人们就不会注意到。我的经验是，当你向人们抱怨数字版权时，除非你向他们介绍所有的来龙去脉及其影响，否则他们只会呆呆地看着。

children.author: Shocka1
children.textRawHtml:
Almost every conversation I've had over the years goes two ways: "Why do you care so much about <Flock, Ring Cameras, big tech name>? If you have nothing to hide, why are you so concerned?"

Or:

"Why do you care so much about <Flock, Ring Cameras, big tech name>? We already walk around with cell phones in our pockets 24/7."

I used to be more passionate about persuading people about privacy, but I've pretty much given up. I've come to the conclusion that the majority of the population doesn't care what is happening around them as long as they have internet access and their iPhone.
children.textZhHtml:
多年来，我经历的几乎每一次对话都分为两种情况： “你为什么这么在意<Flock、Ring摄像头、大型科技公司名称>？如果你没做什么见不得人的事，干嘛这么担心？”

或者：

“你为什么这么在意<Flock、Ring摄像头、大型科技公司名称>？反正我们现在都是24小时手机不离身。”

过去我曾更热衷于劝说人们关注隐私问题，但现在我基本放弃了。我得出的结论是，只要还能上网、还能用iPhone，大多数人根本不在乎周围正在发生什么。

children.author: archontes
children.textRawHtml:
My response is typically, "I have literally everything to hide. I don't want you to know my middle name. I don't want you to know what I had for breakfast. It's none of anyone's business."

And weirdly, the response I get is, "Oh yeah, fair."
children.textZhHtml:
我通常的回答是：“我简直有无数事情要隐瞒。我不想让你知道我的中间名，也不想让你知道我早餐吃了什么。这跟任何人都没关系。”

而奇怪的是，我得到的回应却是：“哦，也是，说得在理。”

stories.title: Postgres rewritten in Rust, now passing 100% of the Postgres regression tests
stories.titleZh: 使用 Rust 重写的 Postgres，现已 100% 通过 Postgres 回归测试

comments.author: malisper
comments.textRawHtml:
Hey author here. Wasn't expecting to see this up.

To concisely give an overview of the project, I've been experimenting with using LLMs to build a better version of Postgres. Postgres is 30 years old and we've learned a lot about databases since hten. A lot of the techniques that work for doing a rewrite are also useful for doing a rearchitecture.

I'm now working on a new, not yet published version of pgrust that incorporates a lot of techniques. Currently the new version:

  - Passes 100% of Postgres regression suite
  - Implements a thread per connection model instead of the process per connection model Postgres does
  - Is 50% faster than Postgres on transaction workloads
  - Is ~300x faster than Postgres on analytical workloads. Right now it's 2x slower than Clickhouse on clickbench and I think it's possible to get faster than Clickhouse
If you have any questions, I'm happy to answer them.
comments.textZhHtml:
嘿，作者。没想到会看到这个。

为了简洁地概述该项目，我一直在尝试使用 LLM 来构建更好的 Postgres 版本。 Postgres 已经有 30 岁了，从那时起我们就学到了很多关于数据库的知识。许多用于重写的技术对于重新架构也很有用。

我现在正在开发一个新的、尚未发布的 prust 版本，它包含了很多技术。目前新版本：

 - 通过 100% Postgres 回归套件
  - 实现每个连接一个线程模型，而不是 Postgres 的每个连接进程模型
  - 在事务工作负载上比 Postgres 快 50%
  - 在分析工作负载上比 Postgres 快大约 300 倍。目前，它在 clickbench 上比 Clickhouse 慢 2 倍，我认为有可能比 Clickhouse 更快
如果您有任何问题，我很乐意回答。

children.author: Keyframe
children.textRawHtml:
I don't want to knock you down as most have already did. In-fact it's a useful exercise going forward in exploring how to work with AI. It's here, we're all going to use it one way or the other. Zero issues with that, in-fact kudos to going through the pain of it all.

Now, having gone through several such endeavors originally myself, albeit with internal tools and systems (as an exercise), I've noticed that while all my tests passed with flying colors the rewrite itself was broken even on basic functionality or missed a ton of details. It was in effect useless when I dived into it. Initial tests also showed massive gain in performance, and I know people who were involved aren't really dumb so something smelled funny. Turns out all those things left out and honestly... moments were the key ingredients.

What I did learn from those beginning explorations though was that one-shotting, grand architecture or source up-front, master plans up-front.. all these do not yield good results - YET. Who know what we'll see in few years though. What I did found that works (FOR ME, nota bene) is to keep the design and checklists for myself, written by myself and then do a small piece by piece.. as if you would if you were coding alone or if you would waterfalling a small team of talented juniors. Then, suddenly super happy results come out, but then it's mostly you driving all the way where llm writes code and offers advice (which for the most part you ignore). It's a happy place for myself at least. It's then truly unlocking yourself to the mythical 10x.

Rewriting a large proven system with decades of ultra expertise behind it, which I don't have, is guaranteed not to end up the same 1:1 replacement. If you found a recipe for that - please do share.
children.textZhHtml:
我不想像大多数人那样把你击倒。事实上，这是探索如何使用人工智能的一个有用的练习。它就在这里，我们都会以一种或另一种方式使用它。零问题，事实上，值得庆幸的是经历了这一切的痛苦。

现在，我自己经历了几次这样的努力，尽管使用了内部工具和系统（作为练习），但我注意到，虽然我的所有测试都顺利通过，但重写本身甚至在基本功能上也被破坏了，或者错过了大量细节。当我深入研究它时，它实际上毫无用处。最初的测试也显示出性能的巨大提升，而且我知道参与其中的人并不傻，所以有些东西闻起来很有趣。事实证明，所有这些被遗漏的东西和老实说......时刻是关键要素。

不过，我从那些开始的探索中确实学到的是一次性的、宏伟的架构或预先的源代码、预先的总体规划..所有这些都不会产生好的结果 - 但。谁知道几年后我们会看到什么。我发现工作（对我来说，nota bene）是为我自己保留设计和清单，由我自己编写，然后一点一点地做......就像你独自编码或如果你要组建一个由才华横溢的初级团队组成的小团队一样。然后，突然超级高兴的结果出来了，但随后主要是你一路开车，llm编写代码并提供建议（大多数情况下你忽略了）。至少对我自己来说这是一个快乐的地方。然后，它真正将您自己解锁到神话般的 10 倍。

重写一个经过验证的大型系统，并在其背后拥有数十年的超级专业知识（而我没有），保证不会最终导致相同的 1:1 替换。如果您找到了一个食谱 - 请分享。

children.author: ramijames
children.textRawHtml: I'm curious. Do you attribute this to weak and/or incomplete tests? How granular should tests be to have complete coverage so that an AI won't create a converted codebase that "passes tests" but is still functionally inaccurate?
children.textZhHtml: 我很好奇。您是否将此归因于薄弱和/或不完整的测试？测试应该有多细粒度才能完全覆盖，这样人工智能就不会创建“通过测试”但功能仍然不准确的转换后的代码库？

children.author: NohatCoder
children.textRawHtml:
There is no such thing as a complete test suite, there will always be some possible bug that it doesn't catch.

In particular, if you put an LLM in an automated loop of "this test fails, please fix it", there is a pretty good chance that it will simply special case all of the tests, possibly in some contrived way that makes it not at all obvious when you read the code.
children.textZhHtml:
不存在绝对完整的测试套件，总会有它无法捕获的潜在漏洞。

特别是，如果你让大语言模型陷入“此测试失败，请修复”的自动化循环中，它很有可能会直接针对所有测试进行特殊处理，甚至可能采用一些生硬的方式，导致你在阅读代码时完全看不出端倪。

children.author: baranul
children.textRawHtml:
This is what I have observed as well. Been through many debates about the "perfect plan" and "perfect tests" fallacies.

Maybe a way of looking at it, to understand the nature of the issue. Have a LLM translate a novel from English to Spanish. Of course it can do that translation at speeds that no human could (score a point for AI). But how good is the Spanish translation? Is the quality better than what humans could do? Wouldn't those who are not fluent in Spanish be more easily impressed?

We then can do all kinds of configuration setups and tests, but how do we know the Spanish was translated perfectly, without a massive detail review (and being already truly bilingual in both English and Spanish)?

As is the usual case in the pursuit of perfection (which nothing in nature ever seems to be), there is going to be mistakes, costs (worth it?), and gray areas. It would be foolhardy for us not to suspect or pass it off as otherwise.
children.textZhHtml:
这也是我的观察。我经历过许多关于“完美计划”和“完美测试”谬误的争论。

也许可以换个角度来看待这个问题，以理解其本质。让大语言模型把一部小说从英语翻译成西班牙语。当然，它能以人类无法企及的速度完成翻译（AI得一分）。但西班牙语翻译的质量如何？比人类做的更好吗？那些不精通西班牙语的人是不是更容易被唬住？

然后我们可以进行各种配置设置和测试，但如果没有大规模的细节审查（并且审查者已经真正精通英语和西班牙语），我们怎么知道西班牙语翻译得完美无缺呢？

正如追求完美时的常见情况（自然界中似乎从未有过完美），总会存在错误、成本（值得吗？）和灰色地带。如果我们不加以怀疑或轻易放过，那就太愚蠢了。

children.author: deanishe
children.textRawHtml:
> how do we know the Spanish was translated perfectly, without a massive detail review

You can't. I think that's a large part of why LLMs have caught on much better with programmers: they have ways of making the computer check its own work.

Checking a document is still a laborious manual task. And completely unfulfilling.
children.textZhHtml:
> 我们怎么知道西班牙语翻译得完美无缺，而没有进行大规模的细节审查呢？

你没法知道。我认为这正是LLM在程序员中更受欢迎的重要原因：他们有办法让计算机自己检查自己的工作。

检查文档仍然是一项繁重的手工任务。而且毫无成就感。

children.author: baranul
children.textRawHtml:
And that's the trap. Relying on a "perfectly" crafted test or a LLM to verify what is unknown. Yes, LLMs can be very useful, but perhaps it's best for us to be realistic.

> making the computer check its own work

Kind of like the Spanish teacher telling his students they can grade their own tests, then being surprised that Billy was always giving himself 100%, when he's nowhere near that bright or fluent.

It wouldn't be so bad, if people were more upfront with being unsure or made it clear they were extrapolating from smaller and limited data. But usually, like many of these unusually cocky LLMs, what is too often reported to the public is "perfection" and many inconvenient truths "swept underneath the carpet".
children.textZhHtml:
而这正是陷阱所在。依赖“完美”设计的测试或LLM来验证未知事物。是的，LLM可能非常有用，但也许我们最好还是现实一点。

> 让计算机自己检查自己的工作

这有点像西班牙语老师告诉学生可以自己批改试卷，然后对比利每次都给自己打满分感到惊讶，可他的聪明才智和语言流利度根本达不到那个水平。

如果人们能更坦诚地表达不确定性，或者明确说明他们只是从较小且有限的数据中推断，情况也不至于这么糟。但通常，就像许多这些异常自负的LLM一样，向公众展示的往往是“完美”，而许多令人不悦的真相则被“扫到了地毯下”。

children.author: aeonfox
children.textRawHtml: This is where fuzzing would be useful.  We have an at-least-parity-bug-level oracle with the reference PostgreSQL implementation.  Just build a generator of queries (both invalid and valid) and ensure the output matches.  The yardstick is how many log10(queries) it can go on average before a discrepancy is found.
children.textZhHtml: 这正是模糊测试能发挥作用的地方。我们有一个至少与参考PostgreSQL实现具有同等缺陷检测能力的预言机。只需构建一个查询生成器（包括无效和有效查询），并确保输出匹配。衡量标准是它在发现差异之前平均能运行多少个log10(queries)。

children.author: apf6
children.textRawHtml: It’s 2026, so another basic technique that every team should add to their testing strategy (in addition to proven techniques like fuzzing) is agentic user simulation. Set up an AI agent with access to the product, and prompt it to use the thing in hundreds of realistic use cases, and to report any possible bugs. It will catch a lot of ‘blind spot’ bugs that were previously things that were only caught by humans.
children.textZhHtml: 现在是2026年，因此每个团队都应该在测试策略中加入另一项基础技术（除了模糊测试等经过验证的技术外），那就是智能体用户模拟。设置一个可以访问产品的AI智能体，提示它在数百个真实用例中使用该产品，并报告任何可能的bug。它能发现许多以前只能靠人类才能发现的“盲点”bug。

children.author: aeonfox
children.textRawHtml:
Debug yourself of the AI hype^. Besides config, SQL implementations have a very limited textual API surface of accepting queries. Putting whole layers of UI and agents around it is really inefficient way of finding edge cases. I wouldn't call that basic, nor the most comprehensive option. Fuzzing at the query layer is far more computationally efficient and effective.

A truly stochastic method is more likely to hit against edge cases, rather than an agent that tends to towards idiomatic solutions and that is trained against a corpus of existing software, and burns millions of tokens/watts spinning its wheels.

^ There's plenty of business value to be found in agentic AI without reaching for it for every solution. I'd even posit agentic AI is even better when paired with focused old-fashioned squishy-brained software engineering in the loop.
children.textZhHtml:
让自己从AI炒作的狂热中清醒过来^。除了配置之外，SQL实现在接受查询方面的文本API交互面非常有限。在它外面包裹上层层叠叠的UI和智能体，是寻找边界情况的一种极其低效的方式。我不会称其为基本方法，也不会认为它是最全面的选择。在查询层进行模糊测试在计算上要高效得多，而且更有效。

真正随机的方法更有可能触及边界情况，而不是那种倾向于采用惯用解决方案、基于现有软件语料库训练，并消耗数百万个token/瓦特电力却原地打转的智能体。

^ 智能体AI蕴含着巨大的商业价值，但这并不意味着每个解决方案都要依赖它。我甚至认为，当智能体AI与专注的、老派的、由人脑主导的传统软件工程相结合并让人类参与闭环时，效果会更好。

children.author: ramijames
children.textRawHtml:
I've always played the game that tests are only as useful as you have resources to attribute to them. Mostly all modern development is a compromise between new features and stuff that supports those new features (tests included, but also reviews, code maintenance, docs, etc.).

If LLMs can be utilized to quickly make deep testing possible, I think that's probably a net-positive.
children.textZhHtml:
我一直认为，测试的效用取决于你能为其投入多少资源。几乎所有现代开发都是在新功能和支持这些新功能的配套工作（包括测试，还有代码审查、代码维护、文档等）之间做出的妥协。

如果能利用大语言模型快速实现深度测试，我认为这总体上可能是利大于弊的。

children.author: cookiengineer
children.textRawHtml:
Unit tests don't test for branch coverage.

That's the culprit, because LLMs tend to forget and remove a lot of branch logic in these kinds of tasks. If unit tests don't cover these specific if/elseif/else cases, then they'll just disappear.

They'll also disappear if the LLM is allowed to modify the unit tests, because they sure like to cheat their way around into greenlit test suites. The agentic environment must disallow write access to the unit test files for the agent that writes the code.

If you implement that in your tools, you'll see quickly how the models will try to rewrite the unit tests at all cost, no matter what kind of prompting you've done. Tool policies are the only boundary to successfully guarantee this.

Source: Am building my own agentic environment because of that behavior
children.textZhHtml:
单元测试不测试分支覆盖率。

这就是罪魁祸首，因为大语言模型在这类任务中往往会遗忘并删除大量分支逻辑。如果单元测试没有覆盖这些特定的if/elseif/else情况，它们就会直接消失。

如果允许大语言模型修改单元测试，它们也会消失，因为它们绝对喜欢通过作弊来让测试套件全部亮绿灯。智能体环境必须禁止编写代码的智能体对单元测试文件的写入权限。

如果你在工具中实现了这一点，你会很快发现模型会不惜一切代价试图重写单元测试，无论你做了怎样的提示。工具策略是唯一能成功保证这一点的边界。

来源：正因为这种行为，我正在构建自己的智能体环境

children.author: film42
children.textRawHtml: A thread per connection is a almost always the correct decision for performance, but by choosing a process per connection, postgres is able to let you load whatever sketchy extensions you want. Worst case you crash the process, not the database. It would be nice if you could strike a balance so a segfaul in the extension only crashes a small percentage of connections, not the whole thing.
children.textZhHtml: 每个连接一个线程对于性能来说几乎总是正确的决定，但是通过为每个连接选择一个进程，postgres 能够让您加载您想要的任何粗略扩展。最坏的情况是进程崩溃，而不是数据库崩溃。如果您能够取得平衡，这样扩展中的段错误只会导致一小部分连接崩溃，而不是整个连接崩溃，那就太好了。

children.author: pilif
children.textRawHtml:
That’s not true for Postgres however: due to its usage of a shared memory pool, whenever a subprocess is terminated unexpectedly, Postgres will kill all other processes and enter recovery mode, replaying the WAL, during which time it will not accept connection requests.

It does this because it can’t possibly know whether the dying process did bad things to the shared memory pool.
children.textZhHtml:
但是对于 Postgres 来说情况并非如此：由于它使用共享内存池，每当子进程意外终止时，Postgres 都会杀死所有其他进程并进入恢复模式，重播 WAL，在此期间它不会接受连接请求。

它这样做是因为它不可能知道垂死的进程是否对共享内存池做了坏事。

children.author: film42
children.textRawHtml: You are correct! TIL, and thank you. Connection processes get a SIGQUIT, shared buffers cleared, and WAL replayed, but postmaster stays alive. It's effectively an online restart.
children.textZhHtml: 你是对的！直到，谢谢你。连接进程收到 SIGQUIT、共享缓冲区被清除、WAL 被重放，但 postmaster 仍保持活动状态。这实际上是一次在线重启。

children.author: anp
children.textRawHtml: I’d be very curious to hear what an online restart approach for sketchy extensions crashing a shared thread per connection process might look like. This is more a question for the author of the project but I’m curious if they have plans in that direction.
children.textZhHtml: 我很好奇，对于导致每个连接进程的共享线程崩溃的粗略扩展的在线重启方法可能是什么样子。这更多是项目作者的问题，但我很好奇他们是否有朝这个方向的计划。

JetStream (not NATS) has a built-in key/value store. Relevant thread (old, from 2023): https://github.com/nats-io/nats.go/discussions/1507

someone did some benchmarks in 2025:

    nats bench kv put --size="128" --msgs 1000000 --storage file
    1m3s → Pub stats: 15,656 msgs/sec ~ 1.91 MB/sec
    
    nats bench kv get --size="128" --msgs 1000000
    59s → Sub stats: 16,720 msgs/sec ~ 2.04 MB/sec
I have personally not used this though.

stories.title: Show HN: Getting GLM 5.2 running on my slow computer
stories.titleZh: 展示 HN：让 GLM 5.2 在我的低配电脑上跑起来

comments.author: lopatin
comments.textRawHtml:
After spending way too much time with Fable a few days ago, I noticed a new hallmark of AI generated text is using the word "honest" everywhere, in a somewhat self congratulating way.

Some things that give it away to me:

- "Honest numbers (WSL2, 12 cores, 25 GB RAM, NVMe via VHDX)"

- "an honest peak projection (working set, KV, MTP row, reconstruction buffers) so the kernel OOM-killer never fires."

- "Honest caveat from the same measurement: ..."

It's the new "It's not X, it's Y". I have no issue with this, I just found it amusing.

Cool project btw!
comments.textZhHtml:
几天前在《神鬼寓言》上花了太多时间后，我注意到人工智能生成的文本的一个新标志是到处使用“诚实”这个词，以某种自我祝贺的方式。

一些事情让我明白了：

-“诚实的数字（WSL2、12 核、25 GB RAM、通过 VHDX 的 NVMe）”

-“诚实的峰值投影（工作集、 KV、MTP 行、重建缓冲区），因此内核 OOM 杀手永远不会触发。”

-“来自同一测量的诚实警告：...”

这是新的“这不是 X，而是 Y”。我对此没有任何问题，我只是觉得它很有趣。

顺便说一句，很酷的项目！

children.author: badatnames
children.textRawHtml: I have almost completely stopped writing real in my own output as a direct consequence of this. Maybe next year they'll catch up with my alternative wordings and I'll end up switching back
children.textZhHtml: 作为这一点的直接后果，我几乎完全停止在自己的输出中写出真实的内容。也许明年他们会赶上我的替代措辞，而我最终会切换回来

children.author: fibonachos
children.textRawHtml:
I’ve started learning into the various AI-isms in my prompts. Most recently, em dashes to chain related information in a single sentence.

“Something something main point — but also pay attention to this related thing — finish main point.”

Anecdotally, it seems to produce more consistent results when I “speak its language”.

On the output side, I’ve become much more aggressive about trimming and editing any prose it produces that I intend on sharing. Both for my own benefit to improve comprehension, but also because dumping the raw, verbose output on my colleagues feels lazy and disrespectful of their time.
children.textZhHtml:
我开始在提示词中研究各种“AI腔调”。最近在用破折号把相关信息串联在一个句子里。

“某某某主要观点——但也要注意这个相关事项——完成主要观点。”

就我个人经验而言，当我“说它的语言”时，似乎能产生更一致的结果。

在输出方面，对于我打算分享的AI生成的文本，我变得更加大刀阔斧地删减和编辑。这既是为了提高我自己的理解效率，也是因为把原始、冗长的输出直接甩给同事显得太敷衍，不尊重他们的时间。

children.author: taneq
children.textRawHtml:
So you’re code switching to incorporate AI-specific ‘vocal fry’ in order to better communicate with it?

That’s not just mimicry. It’s insightful. And it just might work. ;)
children.textZhHtml:
所以你在进行语码转换，融入AI特有的“机械腔调”，以便更好地与它交流？

这不仅仅是模仿。这很有见地。而且说不定真管用。;)

children.author: fibonachos
children.textRawHtml: What’s interesting is that it maps well to how my brain actually works. My chain of thought is very non-linear. Maybe even a little chaotic, going off on tangents before returning to the main thread as it were. This has always made written and verbal communication a bit of a challenge, but the AI seems to parse my unedited stream-of-consciousness without issue.
children.textZhHtml: 有趣的是，这与我大脑的实际运作方式非常契合。我的思维链条非常非线性。甚至可能有点混乱，总是先跑题，然后再回到主线。这一直让我在书面和口头表达上有些吃力，但AI似乎能毫无障碍地解析我未经编辑的意识流。

children.author: lukas9
children.textRawHtml: This is what I've noticed lately. I write articles and over the past year or two I had to stop using specific words or change my writing style a bit many times, because people kept asking if it was AI. It just makes me sad when I show someone my work and the first thing they say is "Is this AI"?
children.textZhHtml: 这也是我最近注意到的现象。我写文章，在过去的一两年里，我不得不多次停止使用某些特定词汇或稍微改变一下写作风格，因为人们总是问这是不是AI写的。当我向别人展示我的作品，而他们开口的第一句话却是“这是AI写的吗？”，这真的让我很难过。

children.author: arximboldi
children.textRawHtml: also substantivizing everything and using "gate", "latch", at every turn. a normal person would say "add the pasta once the water boils" or "the water should be boiling when you add the pasta". Claude would say: water boiling state gates the pasta addition. it sounds obnoxious and engineery but it's so mentally tiring to read and decypher.
children.textZhHtml: 还实体化一切，并在每一个回合使用“门”、“闩”。正常人会说“水沸腾后加入面食”或“加入面食时水应该沸腾”。克劳德会说：水沸腾的状态门控了面食的添加。这听起来很令人讨厌，而且很工程化，但阅读和破译它却让人精神疲惫。

children.author: brettpro
children.textRawHtml: It's been "mathematical" from Gemini for me. Mathematically verified yaml files for Home Assistant, mathematical confirmation of (incorrect) CLI args for a tool, mathematically indistinguishable nutrient profiles of two ingredients.
children.textZhHtml: 双子座对我来说是“数学”的。经过数学验证的家庭助理 yaml 文件，对工具的（错误）CLI 参数进行数学确认，两种成分的数学上无法区分的营养成分。

children.textZhHtml: 我怀疑这是反捏造训练的标志。

children.author: jannyfer
children.textRawHtml:
Yup, and I had seen it in the Opus 4.5 soul document as well: https://gist.github.com/Richard-Weiss/efe157692991535403bd7e...

In the future, I expect different models from different firms behaving differently will become as obviously normal as different humans behaving differently. Those of us that have used agents a lot have already noticed this, but the general public still seems to consider AI to be plug-and-play.

The Opus 4.8 infatuation with being honest genuinely drove me nuts (honest take). The constant need for me to decipher and decide on something after its final message was tiring too.

GPT-5.6-Sol is refreshing that its replies start with "understood." instead of flattering me for my steering prompt. Now ... after a few weeks, will I get suck of "understood"? I dunno :)
children.textZhHtml:
是的，我也在 Opus 4.5 灵魂文档中看到了它：https://gist.github.com/Richard-Weiss/efe157692991535403bd7e...

将来，我预计不同公司的不同模型表现不同将变得像不同的人表现不同一样明显正常。我们这些经常使用代理的人已经注意到了这一点，但公众似乎仍然认为人工智能是即插即用的。

Opus 4.8 对诚实的迷恋确实让我发疯（诚实地说）。在收到最终消息后，我不断地需要破译并做出决定，这也很累人。

GPT-5.6-Sol 令人耳目一新，它的回复以“理解”开头。而不是因为我的转向提示而恭维我。现在……几周后，我会因为“理解”而感到厌烦吗？我不知道:)

children.author: LoganDark
children.textRawHtml: It's as if it's sucking up to me or apologizing at every turn, even when it's done exactly what I've asked. Seems related to sycophancy, but different? (i.e. rather than unconditionally affirming the user it's unconditionally expecting to disappoint)
children.textZhHtml: 就好像它每次都在拍我的马屁或道歉，即使它完全按照我的要求做了。看似与阿谀奉承有关，但又有所不同？ （即，与其无条件肯定用户，不如无条件期待让用户失望）

comments.author: walrus01
comments.textRawHtml:
My main question is whether when put into practical use, this can be measured in tokens/second, or more like 1 token per minute... I have seen locally hosted LLM that are as slow as 1 tok/second still be very useful if you give it a project to do something overnight and metaphorically walk away from it, check back with what it has done in 6 or 8 hours.

0.05 to 0.1 tok/s on the other hand, as reported in the URL for the lowest class of hardware, isn't really usable for much.

edit: I think this is a fantastic project in general concept, and look forward to seeing more efforts towards the general idea of being able to run a 350B to 900B size model locally, even if as slow as 1 tok/s, on hardware that ordinary people can afford. Anything along the general concept of "we have fast read NVME SSD storage, we have a big ass model on local disk, we'll read it at 11GB/tok as we need it, not try to load the whole thing".
comments.textZhHtml:
我的主要问题是，在投入实际使用时，这是否可以以令牌/秒来衡量，或者更像是每分钟 1 个令牌...我见过 慢至 1 tok/秒的本地托管 LLM 仍然非常有用，如果你给它一个项目，让它在一夜之间做某件事，然后隐喻地离开它，检查一下它在 6 或 8 小时内所做的事情。

0.05 到 0.1另一方面，正如 URL 中针对最低级别硬件所报告的那样，tok/s 并没有多大用处。

编辑：我认为这在总体概念上是一个很棒的项目，并期待看到更多的努力实现能够在普通人买得起的硬件上本地运行 350B 到 900B 大小的模型，即使速度慢至 1 tok/s。任何遵循“我们有快速读取 NVME SSD 存储，我们在本地磁盘上有一个大屁股模型，我们将在需要时以 11GB/tok 读取它，而不是尝试加载整个东西”的一般概念的任何内容。

children.author: JumpCrisscross
children.textRawHtml:
The funny thing is Claude Cowork has taught me to be patient with response timelines. I’m now figuring I’ll be running locally no later than 2028.

(I want to spend no more than $10k. And I want to run a model comparable to today’s SOTA.)
children.textZhHtml:
有趣的是 Claude Cowork 教会我对响应时间表要有耐心。我现在预计，我将在 2028 年之前在本地运行。

（我希望花费不超过 1 万美元。而且我希望运行一个与今天的 SOTA 相当的模型。）

children.author: walrus01
children.textRawHtml:
For 10k you can buy a used dual socket Intel or amd based rackmount server with a terabyte of ram, and run models on cpu only at a reasonable speed. Same server would have been 4-5k a couple years ago before ram price rise.

Or buy one on eBay with 512GB that has half its slots populated and then buy the matching 512GB kit to add.
children.textZhHtml:
只需 10k，您就可以购买一台二手双路 Intel 或 AMD 机架式服务器，具有 TB 内存，并且仅以合理的速度在 CPU 上运行模型。几年前，在内存价格上涨之前，同一台服务器的价格可能是 4-5k。

或者在 eBay 上购买一台 512GB 的服务器，其中一半的插槽已填充，然后购买配套的 512GB 套件进行添加。
